+ ARES MANAGEMENT [3 News That Matters]

+ ARES MANAGEMENT (NYSE:ARES) EXPANDS RETAIL PLATFORM WITH US$1.7 BILLION WHITESTONE REIT PURCHASE

Ares Management (NYSE:ARES) has agreed to acquire Whitestone REIT in a transaction valued at US$1.7 billion, taking the retail-focused REIT private and expanding its footprint in necessity-based retail centers across major U.S. metro areas. The deal signals progress in Ares' strategy to deepen exposure to essential consumer needs within real estate and related assets. On Apr. 14, 2026, Ares named E.G. Morse Partner and Head of Asia Credit, and appointed Dinesh Goel and Gabriel Fong to lead the Asia Special Situations team, reflecting the firm's push to grow credit and special situations in Asia. Whitestone's inclusion strengthens Ares' position in retail real estate and aligns with a broader plan to diversify capital allocation across geographies and asset types, including private credit and real assets. Investors will assess how Whitestone integrates with Ares' existing operations and the progress of Asia fundraising as the leadership changes take shape. The deal introduces potential risks such as profit margin compression and higher leverage, while the dividend yield of 5.38% is not fully supported by earnings

+ ARES REAL ESTATE FUNDS TO BUY WHITESTONE REIT AT $19 PER SHARE, VALUED AT ABOUT $1.7 BILLION

On Apr. 10, 2026, Ares Management announced that certain Ares Real Estate funds will acquire all common shares and operating partnership units of Whitestone REIT in an all-cash transaction valued at approximately $1.7 billion, at $19.00 per share or unit. The deal will take Whitestone private and remove the stock from the NYSE. Whitestone’s portfolio includes 56 convenience-focused retail properties totaling roughly 4.9 million square feet in Sun Belt markets, including Phoenix, Austin, Dallas–Fort Worth, Houston and San Antonio. Upon closing, Whitestone will cease trading on the NYSE and operate as a private company. Whitestone CEO Dave Holeman commented, "We believe Whitestone has shown the value of high-return smaller spaces occupied by a well-diversified mix of tenants. Our investment strategy is designed to allow businesses to fuel connection and convenience within thriving, dynamic communities. We believe this transaction with Ares is a testament to the value that strategy has created for our business and, ultimately, for our shareholders." David Roth, global head of real estate strategy and growth at Ares Real Estate, added, "This transaction reflects our high conviction in New Economy real estate as today’s consumers are increasingly seeking convenient experiences for their grocery, pharmacy, healthcare, fitness, and dining needs." The firms noted that the acquisition strengthens Ares’ real estate footprint and aligns with evolving consumer preferences toward convenience and accessibility in retail.

+ ARES MANAGEMENT SECURES ABOUT $5.4 BILLION FOR US AND EUROPE VALUE-ADD REAL ESTATE PUSH

Ares Management Corporation (NYSE:ARES) has raised approximately $5.4 billion for value-add real estate investments in the United States and Europe, as of 9th April 2026.The funds are allocated across two main lines: about $1.9 billion for the EPEP IV fund and roughly $3.5 billion for the U.S. value-add strategy.Initial investments have been identified or deployed totaling around $1.1 billion for each track, reflecting a proactive approach to capturing growth sectors.Analysts are broadly constructive, with about 75% bullish and a consensus price target near $161, implying roughly 60% upside. On 24th March 2026, BMO Capital trimmed its target price for Ares from $140 to $112 and assigned a Market Perform rating, citing concerns over BDC redemptions, asset-based credit issues, and broader market volatility.Ares was founded as a global alternative investment manager. By end of 2025, the firm had attracted significant institutional investment, including BlackRock Inc., which holds about 7.12% stake. The report notes that while Ares presents a solid investment, some investors may seek greater upside in certain AI stocks and other sectors.

+ QATAR AIRWAYS [3 News That Matters]

+ QATAR AIRWAYS CARGO REOPENS UAE ROUTES, EXPANDS FREIGHTER SCHEDULE

Qatar Airways Cargo has resumed freighter and belly operations to and from Doha after a temporary halt due to safety concerns. The updated schedule, published on 28th April 2026, confirms freighter services to Dubai World Central (DWC) twice weekly starting 26th April, and freighter flights to Sharjah (SHJ) three times weekly beginning 1st May. Daily passenger flights to Dubai (DXB) and Sharjah (SHJ) also restart, with the airline planning to lift freighter destinations to over 50 by 1st May and to expand the passenger network to more than 150 destinations by 16th June.Additionally, shipment restrictions have been lifted on certain urgent freight routes such as Victoria, Australia. Starting 1st May, Qatar Airways Cargo will offer products under special handling codes: QRLIV for fish, mammals and diplomatic cargo; and LHO for live human organs and blood. These codes are designed to support time-sensitive cargo while maintaining safety and compliance.Bookings remain available through the Digital Lounge and Qatar Airways Cargo sales teams, while standard sales channels continue to operate. The carrier emphasized that existing products remain accessible during the ramp-up.Analysts and investors are watching the expansion closely as the UAE freight network gains momentum, signaling a broader recovery for regional logistics.This resumption follows earlier safety concerns and underscores the airline's commitment to reviving connectivity and service levels in the Gulf region.

+ QATAR AIRWAYS LAUNCHES NEXT-GEN QSUITE TO LEAD PREMIUM TRAVEL

As of 23rd April 2026, Qatar Airways said the Qsuite Next Generation will debut later this year on the Airbus A350-1000, following delays in the delivery of its new Boeing 777-9 fleet. Originally launched in 2017, the Qsuite has become a hallmark of the airline's premium cabin, with features such as sliding doors, double beds, and modular seating that can accommodate families and small groups. The Next Generation will add a movable 4K OLED screen and enhanced privacy options, while sharpening space management to give travelers more personal space and flexibility during flights. Qatar is positioning the product as part of its broader premium ecosystem at Doha Hamad International Airport, where passengers enjoy dedicated check-in, fast-track security, and access to lounges. The company says the move aims to attract high-yield corporate travelers and affluent leisure passengers and to keep ahead of rivals who are also pursuing luxury cabin innovations.

+ QATAR AIRWAYS BOOSTS GLOBAL REACH AS NETWORK REBUILD GAINS MOMENTUM

Qatar Airways is continuing to lift embargoes and restore its network; by April 20, 2026, passenger operations are back to over 150 destinations, while freighter services now cover more than 50 locations.The restoration is part of a broader strategy to rebuild capacity and meet growing demand in air cargo.However, certain shipments remain restricted. Special handling codes AVF, AVM, and DOC are still embargoed, and live human organs and blood are not accepted.Customers can still book QRLIV shipments under alternate codes, as long as transit time in Doha does not exceed 24 hours.Other products are available via the Digital Lounge and local sales teams, and Qatar Airways Cargo says the network restoration reflects its adaptability. The carrier is lifting embargoes in phases, balancing safety with capacity and aiming to capture a larger share of the growing air cargo market.

value.Title: New Sustainability Research Study Findings Recently Were Reported by Researchers at University of Central Florida (Optimization of the Urban Food-energy-water Nexus: a Micro-supply Chain and Circular Economy Approach)

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-MGB3-SGMP-13KY-00000-00New Sustainability Research Study Findings Recently Were Reported by Researchers at University of Central Florida (Optimization of the Urban Food-energy-water Nexus: a Micro-supply Chain and Circular Economy Approach)2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:45:59ZLexisNexisSUSTAINABILITY RESEARCHNew Sustainability Research Study Findings Recently Were Reported by Researchers at University of Central Florida (Optimization of the Urban Food-energy-water Nexus: a Micro-supply Chain and Circular Economy Approach)


2026 MAY 12 (NewsRx) -- By a News Reporter-Staff News Editor at Economics Daily Report -- Investigators publish new report on Sustainability Research. According to news reporting from Orlando, Florida, by NewsRx journalists, research stated, "This paper presents a mathematical programming model to optimize the design and sustainability performance of the urban food-energy-water (FEW) nexus. The model incorporates a micro supply chain and addresses the supply-demand balance within existing and future FEW systems using performance indicators such as cost and carbon footprint."


The news correspondents obtained a quote from the research from the University of Central Florida, "The problem allows for optimal discrete choices, such as investment in new assets, as well as continuous choices, including capacity of different units and produce exchange among urban farms. The model is applied to an urban agriculture network in South Florida that integrates renewable energy technologies (solar, wind, biomass), combined heat and power (CHP) units, reclaimed wastewater and stormwater for irrigation, and electric vehicles for produce transport. The optimization process identifies the most effective infrastructure investment decisions, resource allocation, and technology configurations to support circular economy practices and long-term sustainability objectives. The proposed framework enables reductions in carbon footprints, food waste, and improves food accessibility in food deserts and strengthens collaboration among urban farms. It supports the planning of resilient urban FEW systems by aligning resource use with social, economic and environmental sustainability objectives."
According to the news reporters, the research concluded: "The results provide a decision-support tool for urban planners and policymakers, offering practical insights to guide infrastructure investment and sustainability planning in other geographic regions."
For more information on this research see: Optimization of the Urban Food-energy-water Nexus: a Micro-supply Chain and Circular Economy Approach. Sustainability, 2026;18(6):2751. Sustainability can be contacted at: Mdpi, Mdpi Ag, Grosspeteranlage 5, Ch-4052 Basel, Switzerland.
Our news journalists report that additional information may be obtained by contacting Marwen Elkamel, University of Central Florida, Ind Engn & Management Syst, Orlando, FL 32816, United States.
The direct object identifier (DOI) for that additional information is: https://doi.org/10.3390/su18062751. This DOI is a link to an online electronic document that is either free or for purchase, and can be your direct source for a journal article and its citation.
Keywords for this news article include: Orlando, Florida, United States, North and Central America, Economies, Investment and Finance, Sustainability Research, University of Central Florida.
Our reports deliver fact-based news of research and discoveries from around the world. Copyright 2026, NewsRx LLC

value.Title: May 05, 2026: Limited U.S.-Flagged Vessels Transit Hormuz as CS Anthem Completes Passage

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK2-7YM3-RS4R-20HV-00000-00May 05, 2026: Limited U.S.-Flagged Vessels Transit Hormuz as CS Anthem Completes Passage2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:45:58ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 05, 2026: Limited U.S.-Flagged Vessels Transit Hormuz as CS Anthem Completes Passage

Crowley Maritime says its CS Anthem completed a transit of the Strait of Hormuz on the 5th of May, 2026, a move that offers visibility into the small set of U.S.-flagged ships departing the Persian Gulf since the crisis began.

Crowley emphasized that crew safety and service continuity are top priorities and thanked U.S. Navy and government partners for their role in maritime safety, while not providing routing or security details.

CS Anthem is one of two U.S.-flagged vessels to transit under Project Freedom, along with Alliance Fairfax of Maersk; both left the region under military escort.

The transit underscores ongoing risk environment; MSP/TSP vessels remain part of crisis logistics, ensuring that critical U.S.-flagged capacity remains available for military support when needed.

INDEX

SECTION 1 CROWLEY MARITIME PROFILE

SECTION 2 CROWLEY MARITIME TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 3 CROWLEY MARITIME RANKING

SECTION 1 CROWLEY MARITIME PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

value.Title: CROWLEY MARITIME - THE RECENT SECTOR NEWS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-DVG3-S2B7-8410-00000-00CROWLEY MARITIME - THE RECENT SECTOR NEWS2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:34ZLexisNexisSTOCKCROWLEY MARITIME - THE RECENT SECTOR NEWS

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

PermID4295904276Websitehttp://www.crowley.com/IndustryMaritime & Shipping Logistics

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ CROWLEY MARITIME EXPANDS INTERESTS AT PORT MCKENZIE WITH NEW FUEL TERMINAL STUDY

Crowley Maritime is set to capitalize on the increasing interest surrounding Port McKenzie by securing a permit to study the development of a large fuel terminal. This strategic move could significantly enhance the company's operational footprint in Alaska's Matanuska-Susitna Borough.On the 7th of January 2026, Crowley Maritime announced its plans to conduct a comprehensive study aimed at establishing a fuel terminal at Port McKenzie. The initiative comes amid rising commercial activities and interest in the region, which is poised to benefit from enhanced logistics and transportation capabilities.The proposed fuel terminal at Port McKenzie is expected to play a critical role in supporting local industries and businesses by providing a reliable fuel supply chain. Crowley Maritime's investment in this infrastructure is seen as a response to the growing demand for fuel in both marine and land-based operations in Alaska, a region that has been increasingly recognized for its potential in various sectors.As part of its strategic growth plan, Crowley Maritime is focusing on expanding its services in Alaska, leveraging the advantageous location of Port McKenzie. The terminal is anticipated to not only streamline operations for Crowley but also attract other businesses looking to establish a presence in the region, further enhancing the economic landscape of the Matanuska-Susitna Borough.With the approval of this permit, Crowley Maritime is now positioned to move forward with its feasibility studies and engage with local stakeholders. The development of the fuel terminal could mark a significant milestone for both Crowley and Port McKenzie, potentially paving the way for future investments and growth in the area.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME RESTRUCTURES LEADERSHIP TO DRIVE GROWTH IN SHIPPING AND ENERGY

Crowley Maritime has announced a strategic consolidation of its business structure aimed at enhancing efficiency and value for customers. On 6th January 2026, the company revealed the establishment of two new divisions: Shipping and Logistics, led by Executive Vice President and Division President James C. Fowler, and Energy, overseen by Executive Vice President and Division President Kollin Fencil. This reorganization is designed to improve operational execution and agility, positioning Crowley Maritime for accelerated growth across its shipping, logistics, and energy sectors.The Shipping and Logistics Division, under Fowler's leadership, merges Crowley's logistics, land transportation, and global ship management businesses. This division is set to serve both commercial and government customers, providing a comprehensive array of ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transportation, and warehousing solutions. Fowler emphasized that this new structure will ensure that the entire transportation value chain for their customers is managed cohesively, ultimately driving new growth through safe and efficient supply chain solutions.Meanwhile, the Energy Division, guided by Fencil, consolidates operations including Crowley Fuels in Alaska, liquefied natural gas (LNG) services, and advanced energy solutions. Fencil noted that the division's new structure enables Crowley Maritime to innovate and scale more effectively within traditional and emerging energy markets. With a long history of supporting diverse energy needs across the U.S., the realignment is expected to further enhance Crowley's commitment to meeting evolving customer demands for both traditional fuels and advanced energy solutions.Both Fowler and Fencil bring significant experience to their new roles, having joined Crowley Maritime as senior vice presidents in 2023. Under their respective leadership, the company has seen improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency within their business units. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson remarked that this realignment empowers faster decision-making and enhances the company's ability to adapt to market demands, ensuring that Crowley Maritime remains a leader in the maritime, energy, and logistics sectors.Crowley Maritime, a privately held U.S.-owned company, has been providing innovative transportation services for over 130 years. With operations spanning 36 nations and territories, the company is recognized as one of the leading employers of U.S. mariners. As Crowley Maritime embarks on this new chapter, stakeholders will be closely monitoring the impact of these changes on the company's growth trajectory and operational capabilities.

+ CROWLEY AND SEACOR JOIN FORCES IN NEW JONES ACT TANKER VENTURE

Crowley Maritime and SEACOR Holdings, through its subsidiary Seabulk Tankers, have formed a joint venture called Fairwater Holdings to integrate their liquid energy and chemical transportation assets. This collaboration aims to address the evolving demands of the U.S. domestic market. The agreement was finalized on 2nd January 2026, establishing Fairwater Holdings as an independent U.S. Jones Act service provider in the maritime sector.The new entity will amalgamate the strengths of both companies, providing a suite of transportation solutions across the petroleum and chemical trades, alongside third-party ship management services. Fairwater Holdings will manage a fleet comprising 20 ocean-going articulated tug-barges and 11 tankers, primarily under long-term charters. Furthermore, the joint venture will offer crewing and technical management for an additional 21 vessels owned by third parties, thereby enhancing its operational capacity and service offerings.Daniel Thorogood, currently the CEO of Seabulk, will take on the CEO role at Fairwater Holdings upon completion of the deal. With over a decade of experience leading Seabulk and managing SEACOR's short-sea shipping and logistics investments, Thorogood is expected to steer the new venture towards innovation and sustainable practices. In his statement, he emphasized that Fairwater marks a significant milestone in delivering safe and efficient maritime transportation solutions tailored to the needs of the U.S. energy sector.Tom Crowley, Chairman and CEO of Crowley Maritime, expressed confidence in the partnership, describing Seabulk as an ideal collaborator with a proven track record in the maritime industry. He noted that the joint venture aligns with Crowley's growth strategy, further solidifying its position as a leader in the U.S. maritime market, where it operates the largest American-flag petroleum and chemical tank vessel fleet, boasting a capacity exceeding 12 million barrels.As both companies work towards integrating their assets and services, the joint venture is expected to commence operations after meeting customary closing conditions and securing regulatory approvals. This integration effort is anticipated to create a unified and seamless transition for customers, ensuring uninterrupted support for their energy supply chains. Fairwater Holdings is set to begin operations in the first quarter of 2024, marking an exciting new chapter in U.S. maritime transportation.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ MAWANI STRENGTHENS TRADE TIES WITH CMA CGM’S NEW ROUTE

RIYADH: Maritime connectivity between Saudi Arabia and key Asian and European markets is set to expand after the Kingdom added CMA CGM’s 'Ocean Rise' shipping service to Jeddah Islamic Port. This new route, which commenced on the 3rd of May 2026, connects Jeddah Islamic Port with Kobe, Nagoya, and Yokohama in Japan; Xiamen, Yantian, and Nansha in China; Rotterdam in the Netherlands; Hamburg in Germany; and Southampton in the UK.According to Saudi Arabia’s Ports Authority, known as Mawani, the service has a handling capacity of up to 3,001 twenty-foot equivalent units (TEUs) and is aimed at supporting the movement of Saudi exports and imports in cooperation with international shipping lines. This addition is part of Mawani’s ongoing strategy to enhance the Kingdom’s global logistics capabilities.Mawani stated that this new service aligns with its efforts to enhance Saudi Arabia’s ranking in global performance indicators and support global trade flows. The move is in line with the objectives of the National Transport and Logistics Strategy, which aims to reinforce the Kingdom’s standing as a global logistics hub connecting three continents.During the current period, Mawani has launched 18 maritime shipping services, including the latest CMA CGM offering, with a combined estimated handling capacity of 123,552 TEUs. This expansion aims to ensure efficient delivery of goods to markets and enhance operational readiness across Saudi ports in cooperation with key global shipping companies, including MSC, Maersk, PIL, and Hapag-Lloyd.The Kingdom’s port activity has continued to expand, with Saudi ports recording a 6.66 percent annual increase in cargo throughput in March. Mawani-overseen ports handled 667,882 TEUs in February, marking a significant 20.89 percent increase from the same month a year earlier. Jeddah Islamic Port remains one of the Kingdom’s key maritime gateways, equipped with advanced facilities to bolster its capacity for international trade.

+ CMA CGM EXPANDS FLEET WITH TWO MORE VESSELS REGISTERED IN INDIA

CMACGM is transferring two more containerships into the Indian registry, according to officials in India. This development was reported by the Maritime Executive on April 29th, 2026, and marks an important step in the shipping company's strategy to enhance its presence in the Indian market.The shipping giant began moving vessels under the Indian flag last year to align with revised cabotage regulations. Earlier in April 2026, the CMA CGM Vila do Conde, a 33,434-dwt vessel built in 2009 with a capacity of 2,556 TEU, was officially transferred from the Maltese flag to India. This vessel has been part of CMACGM's fleet since 2023.Papers have also been filed for the transfer of the CMA CGM Semarang, a vessel constructed in 2007 with a deadweight tonnage of 38,000 and a capacity of 2,700 TEU. These two new transfers will bring the total number of vessels moved into the Indian registry by CMACGM to six, following the earlier transfers of the CMA CGM Victoria, CMA CGM Mendelssohn, CMA CGM Diamond, and CMA CGM Manaus.This initiative is largely a response to India's recent changes in cabotage regulations, which now restrict foreign-flagged vessels from carrying domestic cargo. Since January 2026, the Ministry of Ports, Shipping, and Waterways has rolled back a previous exemption that permitted foreign vessels to transship certain cargoes. CMA CGM's proactive approach to register vessels in India allows the company to navigate these new rules effectively while bolstering the domestic shipping industry.CMA CGM was the first major carrier to transition vessels into the Indian registry, with competitors like Maersk and MSC Mediterranean Shipping Company following suit. The new regulations also necessitate that registered vessels employ Indian seafarers, a move CMA CGM has already begun, employing 1,000 Indian seafarers with plans to increase this number to 1,500 by year-end. Furthermore, CMA CGM signed a contract with Cochin Shipyard in February 2026 for the construction of new vessels powered by LNG, reflecting its commitment to growth and sustainability in the region.

+ CMACGM'S AI-FIRST APPROACH TO REDEFINING INDIA'S LOGISTICS LANDSCAPE

As supply chains grow more volatile and customer expectations more exacting, logistics is undergoing a profound reinvention. CMA CGM Group outlines how India is emerging as both a strategic growth market and a global innovation hub, while AI, ecosystem partnerships, and digital platforms are redefining everything from port operations to customer experience. The result: a shift from reactive logistics to predictive, intelligent, and increasingly autonomous supply chains.On 28th April 2026, CMA CGM highlighted its commitment to harnessing artificial intelligence to enhance operational efficiencies within the logistics sector. The company is focusing on developing AI-driven solutions that optimize supply chain management, thereby minimizing disruptions caused by unpredictable market dynamics. By leveraging advanced analytics and machine learning, CMA CGM aims to create a more resilient logistics framework that meets the evolving demands of its customers.CMACGM's strategy involves forging partnerships with Indian tech companies to build a robust ecosystem that supports the integration of AI into logistics operations. This collaborative approach not only accelerates innovation but also positions India as a critical player in the global logistics landscape. The company's vision is to establish AI as a core component of its operational model, facilitating smarter decision-making and improved customer service.In addition to AI integration, CMA CGM is investing in digital platforms that enhance transparency and facilitate real-time tracking of shipments. This technological advancement empowers businesses to make informed decisions, thereby improving overall supply chain performance. As CMACGM continues to evolve its logistics capabilities, the emphasis is placed on creating a seamless experience for clients, ultimately driving growth in the e-commerce and manufacturing sectors.With CMA CGM at the forefront of this transformation, the logistics industry is poised for significant change. The company's focus on an AI-first strategy underscores the importance of innovation in maintaining competitive advantage. As global trade continues to evolve, CMACGM's initiatives in India may serve as a blueprint for other logistics providers aiming to navigate the complexities of modern supply chains.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

value.Title: Trump Has Created a Climate Opportunity
value.Overview: The American Prospect, CLIMATE CHANGE NEWS, 1225words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-SCH3-RYHX-71NN-00000-00Trump Has Created a Climate Opportunity2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:39:51ZLexisNexisCLIMATE CHANGE NEWSTrump Has Created a Climate OpportunityRyan Cooper

In the second Trump administration, Democrats have been backing away from climate change messaging. Joe Biden apparently got no credit for the Inflation Reduction Act, his marquee climate policy bill, and Trump has since unceremoniously disposed of it.

The Searchlight Institute, a centrist think tank, presented polling last September indicating that while most Americans think climate change is a problem, they don’t think it’s a major one. Therefore, the first step to solving climate change is “don’t say climate change.” Luckily, as Matt Huber points out at The New York Times, tremendous progress in renewable energy means one can accomplish a lot, emissions-wise, without mentioning climate change at all. The “heart of any affordability agenda—housing, energy, transportation—overlaps with the sectors we must decarbonize,” he writes. “When it comes to climate change, for now, it might be better to say nothing at all.”

More from Ryan Cooper

When it comes to actual politicians running for office this year, perhaps that is a sensible strategy. But in terms of the rest of the broad Democratic coalition, it is not.

It’s not hard to see why climate policy polls poorly: People have been comprehensively misled about it. On one hand, climate activism emerged from the environmental movement, which tends to present measures to protect the environment as posing an inherent trade-off with jobs and growth. If we create some new national park, for instance, then that land won’t be available for aluminum smelters or data centers. (In reality, it is usually not this simple, but that’s the stereotype.)

On the other hand, and much more importantly, right-wing media—heavily funded by fossil fuel interests—has been lying about climate change for decades. They lied for years that it wasn’t happening, then they lied for years that it was “paused,” and now that the problem is undeniably happening all around us, they lie that we can’t do anything about it.

The result is that for the median voter, climate change is seen as a problem but one that will be burdensome to solve. It will mean higher gas and electricity prices, having to buy an expensive electric car, spending tax dollars on infrastructure upgrades, and so on. When times are tough, climate slips down the priority list.

This way of thinking, of course, is not remotely true. Climate change is going to wreck the environment and the economy—indeed, it is already happening. The home insurance markets in Florida and California are in death spirals. Increasingly severe heat waves are killing people and straining the electric grid all across the country. Flooding strikes in more places, and more severely. The same is true of drought. The Colorado River Basin just suffered its driest winter in hundreds of years. The water level on the Colorado’s Lake Powell, the second-largest reservoir in the country, might sink below its power inlet tubesthis summer, and it will almost certainly have to be decommissioned within the next decade.

Conversely, it is now beyond any question that the 21st-century economy will be driven by renewable energy. It is the cheapest form of energy in history, and whoever can figure out how to power their whole economy with it will reap a tremendous harvest of prosperity and health, as the pollution from filth-spewing fossil energy gradually clears.

In a weird stroke of good fortune, Trump’s lunatic misrule provides a perfect context to press the correct argument about climate change: that doing so will help every part of America, from the environment to the economy writ large to Jeffrey Q. Consumer, struggling to pay his bills. Thanks to Trump’s war on Iran having closed the Strait of Hormuz, gas prices are shooting through the roof, followed quickly by food and electricity prices. And thanks to his repeal of the Inflation Reduction Act, China has cemented a massive lead in the technology and industry of the future. They almost can’t believe their luck; a Chinese university recently published a stock-take of his policies in 2025 entitled “Thank Trump.”

It is true that you don’t have to talk about climate change specifically to justify a major climate package. Renewable energy is being deployed at scale, even in the U.S., and EVs are less expensive to run over time because of cheaper fueling and few maintenance requirements. Indeed, in a rapidly growing fraction of the world, EVs are cheaper to purchase up front, too. China is showing how to decarbonize trucking.

But decarbonizing electricity production and transport is not enough. We also need to decarbonize industry and agriculture, and strategies for doing so are not all ready for deployment, particularly in the latter category. As I wrote back during the 2024 campaign, Joe Biden got us maybe halfway where we needed to be with the IRA. Now we don’t even have that foundation to build on.

In short, an immense package of investment and research will be needed to reach the (literally) sunlit uplands where fossil fuels are not needed for anything, aside from maybe chemical production. Given the macroeconomic conditions likely to prevail in 2029—which might be a severe case of stagflation at the rate we’re going, if not a debt crisis—it will most probably be quite painful and disruptive. The American people must be convinced the price is worth it.

That brings me to my most fundamental disagreement with this style of politics: Muzzling yourself because certain topics momentarily don’t poll well is strategically shortsighted and against basic small-d democratic values. As Steve Randy Waldman argues, any democratic system is premised on the idea that politicians and political parties will make honest arguments about what they think should happen. That’s how voters can trust that their choice in an election will be a meaningful one and thereby come to understand the effect of what voting for particular parties will do, which doesn’t happen when parties yank their policies back and forth based on what (invariably biased) polls happen to come in with. “When political parties reshape themselves on the basis of polling, it renders the whole process nonsensical, a kind of infinite regress,” he writes. “It deprives the public of meaningful choice in the name of giving it what it wants.”

More concretely, consider the Gallup poll of approval of interracial marriage. Back in 1954, it came in at just 4 percent. By 2021, that had risen to 94 percent. Why the change? Because generations of ordinary people, activists, and even some politicians—knowing full well they were charging headlong into a storm of public condemnation—argued that in a free country, people ought to be allowed to marry whoever they damn well want.

If civil rights activists had thought like today’s poll-sniffing political consultants, they would have carefully avoided any such hideously unpopular ideas. They would have yelled at Richard and Mildred Loving to avoid raising the dreaded salience of the issue by appealing their conviction for violating Virginia’s anti-miscegenation law to the Supreme Court, no matter how eerily apropos their surname might be. “What are you trying to do, elect more Dixiecrats?” consultants would have bellowed. And interracial marriage might still be illegal to this day.

The post Trump Has Created a Climate Opportunity appeared first on The American Prospect.

value.Title: CROWLEY MARITIME - THE RECENT SECTOR UPDATES WITH PEERS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-FNH3-S0MP-43TV-00000-00CROWLEY MARITIME - THE RECENT SECTOR UPDATES WITH PEERS2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:34ZLexisNexisSTOCKCROWLEY MARITIME - THE RECENT SECTOR UPDATES WITH PEERS

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

PermID4295904276Websitehttp://www.crowley.com/IndustryMaritime & Shipping Logistics

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ CROWLEY MARITIME EXPANDS INTERESTS AT PORT MCKENZIE WITH NEW FUEL TERMINAL STUDY

Crowley Maritime is set to capitalize on the increasing interest surrounding Port McKenzie by securing a permit to study the development of a large fuel terminal. This strategic move could significantly enhance the company's operational footprint in Alaska's Matanuska-Susitna Borough.On the 7th of January 2026, Crowley Maritime announced its plans to conduct a comprehensive study aimed at establishing a fuel terminal at Port McKenzie. The initiative comes amid rising commercial activities and interest in the region, which is poised to benefit from enhanced logistics and transportation capabilities.The proposed fuel terminal at Port McKenzie is expected to play a critical role in supporting local industries and businesses by providing a reliable fuel supply chain. Crowley Maritime's investment in this infrastructure is seen as a response to the growing demand for fuel in both marine and land-based operations in Alaska, a region that has been increasingly recognized for its potential in various sectors.As part of its strategic growth plan, Crowley Maritime is focusing on expanding its services in Alaska, leveraging the advantageous location of Port McKenzie. The terminal is anticipated to not only streamline operations for Crowley but also attract other businesses looking to establish a presence in the region, further enhancing the economic landscape of the Matanuska-Susitna Borough.With the approval of this permit, Crowley Maritime is now positioned to move forward with its feasibility studies and engage with local stakeholders. The development of the fuel terminal could mark a significant milestone for both Crowley and Port McKenzie, potentially paving the way for future investments and growth in the area.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME RESTRUCTURES LEADERSHIP TO DRIVE GROWTH IN SHIPPING AND ENERGY

Crowley Maritime has announced a strategic consolidation of its business structure aimed at enhancing efficiency and value for customers. On 6th January 2026, the company revealed the establishment of two new divisions: Shipping and Logistics, led by Executive Vice President and Division President James C. Fowler, and Energy, overseen by Executive Vice President and Division President Kollin Fencil. This reorganization is designed to improve operational execution and agility, positioning Crowley Maritime for accelerated growth across its shipping, logistics, and energy sectors.The Shipping and Logistics Division, under Fowler's leadership, merges Crowley's logistics, land transportation, and global ship management businesses. This division is set to serve both commercial and government customers, providing a comprehensive array of ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transportation, and warehousing solutions. Fowler emphasized that this new structure will ensure that the entire transportation value chain for their customers is managed cohesively, ultimately driving new growth through safe and efficient supply chain solutions.Meanwhile, the Energy Division, guided by Fencil, consolidates operations including Crowley Fuels in Alaska, liquefied natural gas (LNG) services, and advanced energy solutions. Fencil noted that the division's new structure enables Crowley Maritime to innovate and scale more effectively within traditional and emerging energy markets. With a long history of supporting diverse energy needs across the U.S., the realignment is expected to further enhance Crowley's commitment to meeting evolving customer demands for both traditional fuels and advanced energy solutions.Both Fowler and Fencil bring significant experience to their new roles, having joined Crowley Maritime as senior vice presidents in 2023. Under their respective leadership, the company has seen improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency within their business units. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson remarked that this realignment empowers faster decision-making and enhances the company's ability to adapt to market demands, ensuring that Crowley Maritime remains a leader in the maritime, energy, and logistics sectors.Crowley Maritime, a privately held U.S.-owned company, has been providing innovative transportation services for over 130 years. With operations spanning 36 nations and territories, the company is recognized as one of the leading employers of U.S. mariners. As Crowley Maritime embarks on this new chapter, stakeholders will be closely monitoring the impact of these changes on the company's growth trajectory and operational capabilities.

+ CROWLEY AND SEACOR JOIN FORCES IN NEW JONES ACT TANKER VENTURE

Crowley Maritime and SEACOR Holdings, through its subsidiary Seabulk Tankers, have formed a joint venture called Fairwater Holdings to integrate their liquid energy and chemical transportation assets. This collaboration aims to address the evolving demands of the U.S. domestic market. The agreement was finalized on 2nd January 2026, establishing Fairwater Holdings as an independent U.S. Jones Act service provider in the maritime sector.The new entity will amalgamate the strengths of both companies, providing a suite of transportation solutions across the petroleum and chemical trades, alongside third-party ship management services. Fairwater Holdings will manage a fleet comprising 20 ocean-going articulated tug-barges and 11 tankers, primarily under long-term charters. Furthermore, the joint venture will offer crewing and technical management for an additional 21 vessels owned by third parties, thereby enhancing its operational capacity and service offerings.Daniel Thorogood, currently the CEO of Seabulk, will take on the CEO role at Fairwater Holdings upon completion of the deal. With over a decade of experience leading Seabulk and managing SEACOR's short-sea shipping and logistics investments, Thorogood is expected to steer the new venture towards innovation and sustainable practices. In his statement, he emphasized that Fairwater marks a significant milestone in delivering safe and efficient maritime transportation solutions tailored to the needs of the U.S. energy sector.Tom Crowley, Chairman and CEO of Crowley Maritime, expressed confidence in the partnership, describing Seabulk as an ideal collaborator with a proven track record in the maritime industry. He noted that the joint venture aligns with Crowley's growth strategy, further solidifying its position as a leader in the U.S. maritime market, where it operates the largest American-flag petroleum and chemical tank vessel fleet, boasting a capacity exceeding 12 million barrels.As both companies work towards integrating their assets and services, the joint venture is expected to commence operations after meeting customary closing conditions and securing regulatory approvals. This integration effort is anticipated to create a unified and seamless transition for customers, ensuring uninterrupted support for their energy supply chains. Fairwater Holdings is set to begin operations in the first quarter of 2024, marking an exciting new chapter in U.S. maritime transportation.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ MAWANI STRENGTHENS TRADE TIES WITH CMA CGM’S NEW ROUTE

RIYADH: Maritime connectivity between Saudi Arabia and key Asian and European markets is set to expand after the Kingdom added CMA CGM’s 'Ocean Rise' shipping service to Jeddah Islamic Port. This new route, which commenced on the 3rd of May 2026, connects Jeddah Islamic Port with Kobe, Nagoya, and Yokohama in Japan; Xiamen, Yantian, and Nansha in China; Rotterdam in the Netherlands; Hamburg in Germany; and Southampton in the UK.According to Saudi Arabia’s Ports Authority, known as Mawani, the service has a handling capacity of up to 3,001 twenty-foot equivalent units (TEUs) and is aimed at supporting the movement of Saudi exports and imports in cooperation with international shipping lines. This addition is part of Mawani’s ongoing strategy to enhance the Kingdom’s global logistics capabilities.Mawani stated that this new service aligns with its efforts to enhance Saudi Arabia’s ranking in global performance indicators and support global trade flows. The move is in line with the objectives of the National Transport and Logistics Strategy, which aims to reinforce the Kingdom’s standing as a global logistics hub connecting three continents.During the current period, Mawani has launched 18 maritime shipping services, including the latest CMA CGM offering, with a combined estimated handling capacity of 123,552 TEUs. This expansion aims to ensure efficient delivery of goods to markets and enhance operational readiness across Saudi ports in cooperation with key global shipping companies, including MSC, Maersk, PIL, and Hapag-Lloyd.The Kingdom’s port activity has continued to expand, with Saudi ports recording a 6.66 percent annual increase in cargo throughput in March. Mawani-overseen ports handled 667,882 TEUs in February, marking a significant 20.89 percent increase from the same month a year earlier. Jeddah Islamic Port remains one of the Kingdom’s key maritime gateways, equipped with advanced facilities to bolster its capacity for international trade.

+ CMA CGM EXPANDS FLEET WITH TWO MORE VESSELS REGISTERED IN INDIA

CMACGM is transferring two more containerships into the Indian registry, according to officials in India. This development was reported by the Maritime Executive on April 29th, 2026, and marks an important step in the shipping company's strategy to enhance its presence in the Indian market.The shipping giant began moving vessels under the Indian flag last year to align with revised cabotage regulations. Earlier in April 2026, the CMA CGM Vila do Conde, a 33,434-dwt vessel built in 2009 with a capacity of 2,556 TEU, was officially transferred from the Maltese flag to India. This vessel has been part of CMACGM's fleet since 2023.Papers have also been filed for the transfer of the CMA CGM Semarang, a vessel constructed in 2007 with a deadweight tonnage of 38,000 and a capacity of 2,700 TEU. These two new transfers will bring the total number of vessels moved into the Indian registry by CMACGM to six, following the earlier transfers of the CMA CGM Victoria, CMA CGM Mendelssohn, CMA CGM Diamond, and CMA CGM Manaus.This initiative is largely a response to India's recent changes in cabotage regulations, which now restrict foreign-flagged vessels from carrying domestic cargo. Since January 2026, the Ministry of Ports, Shipping, and Waterways has rolled back a previous exemption that permitted foreign vessels to transship certain cargoes. CMA CGM's proactive approach to register vessels in India allows the company to navigate these new rules effectively while bolstering the domestic shipping industry.CMA CGM was the first major carrier to transition vessels into the Indian registry, with competitors like Maersk and MSC Mediterranean Shipping Company following suit. The new regulations also necessitate that registered vessels employ Indian seafarers, a move CMA CGM has already begun, employing 1,000 Indian seafarers with plans to increase this number to 1,500 by year-end. Furthermore, CMA CGM signed a contract with Cochin Shipyard in February 2026 for the construction of new vessels powered by LNG, reflecting its commitment to growth and sustainability in the region.

+ CMACGM'S AI-FIRST APPROACH TO REDEFINING INDIA'S LOGISTICS LANDSCAPE

As supply chains grow more volatile and customer expectations more exacting, logistics is undergoing a profound reinvention. CMA CGM Group outlines how India is emerging as both a strategic growth market and a global innovation hub, while AI, ecosystem partnerships, and digital platforms are redefining everything from port operations to customer experience. The result: a shift from reactive logistics to predictive, intelligent, and increasingly autonomous supply chains.On 28th April 2026, CMA CGM highlighted its commitment to harnessing artificial intelligence to enhance operational efficiencies within the logistics sector. The company is focusing on developing AI-driven solutions that optimize supply chain management, thereby minimizing disruptions caused by unpredictable market dynamics. By leveraging advanced analytics and machine learning, CMA CGM aims to create a more resilient logistics framework that meets the evolving demands of its customers.CMACGM's strategy involves forging partnerships with Indian tech companies to build a robust ecosystem that supports the integration of AI into logistics operations. This collaborative approach not only accelerates innovation but also positions India as a critical player in the global logistics landscape. The company's vision is to establish AI as a core component of its operational model, facilitating smarter decision-making and improved customer service.In addition to AI integration, CMA CGM is investing in digital platforms that enhance transparency and facilitate real-time tracking of shipments. This technological advancement empowers businesses to make informed decisions, thereby improving overall supply chain performance. As CMACGM continues to evolve its logistics capabilities, the emphasis is placed on creating a seamless experience for clients, ultimately driving growth in the e-commerce and manufacturing sectors.With CMA CGM at the forefront of this transformation, the logistics industry is poised for significant change. The company's focus on an AI-first strategy underscores the importance of innovation in maintaining competitive advantage. As global trade continues to evolve, CMACGM's initiatives in India may serve as a blueprint for other logistics providers aiming to navigate the complexities of modern supply chains.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

value.Title: CROWLEY MARITIME - THE RECENT NEWS IN THE SECTOR AND ITS PEERS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-N343-RTCX-44Y8-00000-00CROWLEY MARITIME - THE RECENT NEWS IN THE SECTOR AND ITS PEERS2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:34ZLexisNexisSTOCKCROWLEY MARITIME - THE RECENT NEWS IN THE SECTOR AND ITS PEERS

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

PermID4295904276Websitehttp://www.crowley.com/IndustryMaritime & Shipping Logistics

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ CROWLEY MARITIME EXPANDS INTERESTS AT PORT MCKENZIE WITH NEW FUEL TERMINAL STUDY

Crowley Maritime is set to capitalize on the increasing interest surrounding Port McKenzie by securing a permit to study the development of a large fuel terminal. This strategic move could significantly enhance the company's operational footprint in Alaska's Matanuska-Susitna Borough.On the 7th of January 2026, Crowley Maritime announced its plans to conduct a comprehensive study aimed at establishing a fuel terminal at Port McKenzie. The initiative comes amid rising commercial activities and interest in the region, which is poised to benefit from enhanced logistics and transportation capabilities.The proposed fuel terminal at Port McKenzie is expected to play a critical role in supporting local industries and businesses by providing a reliable fuel supply chain. Crowley Maritime's investment in this infrastructure is seen as a response to the growing demand for fuel in both marine and land-based operations in Alaska, a region that has been increasingly recognized for its potential in various sectors.As part of its strategic growth plan, Crowley Maritime is focusing on expanding its services in Alaska, leveraging the advantageous location of Port McKenzie. The terminal is anticipated to not only streamline operations for Crowley but also attract other businesses looking to establish a presence in the region, further enhancing the economic landscape of the Matanuska-Susitna Borough.With the approval of this permit, Crowley Maritime is now positioned to move forward with its feasibility studies and engage with local stakeholders. The development of the fuel terminal could mark a significant milestone for both Crowley and Port McKenzie, potentially paving the way for future investments and growth in the area.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME RESTRUCTURES LEADERSHIP TO DRIVE GROWTH IN SHIPPING AND ENERGY

Crowley Maritime has announced a strategic consolidation of its business structure aimed at enhancing efficiency and value for customers. On 6th January 2026, the company revealed the establishment of two new divisions: Shipping and Logistics, led by Executive Vice President and Division President James C. Fowler, and Energy, overseen by Executive Vice President and Division President Kollin Fencil. This reorganization is designed to improve operational execution and agility, positioning Crowley Maritime for accelerated growth across its shipping, logistics, and energy sectors.The Shipping and Logistics Division, under Fowler's leadership, merges Crowley's logistics, land transportation, and global ship management businesses. This division is set to serve both commercial and government customers, providing a comprehensive array of ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transportation, and warehousing solutions. Fowler emphasized that this new structure will ensure that the entire transportation value chain for their customers is managed cohesively, ultimately driving new growth through safe and efficient supply chain solutions.Meanwhile, the Energy Division, guided by Fencil, consolidates operations including Crowley Fuels in Alaska, liquefied natural gas (LNG) services, and advanced energy solutions. Fencil noted that the division's new structure enables Crowley Maritime to innovate and scale more effectively within traditional and emerging energy markets. With a long history of supporting diverse energy needs across the U.S., the realignment is expected to further enhance Crowley's commitment to meeting evolving customer demands for both traditional fuels and advanced energy solutions.Both Fowler and Fencil bring significant experience to their new roles, having joined Crowley Maritime as senior vice presidents in 2023. Under their respective leadership, the company has seen improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency within their business units. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson remarked that this realignment empowers faster decision-making and enhances the company's ability to adapt to market demands, ensuring that Crowley Maritime remains a leader in the maritime, energy, and logistics sectors.Crowley Maritime, a privately held U.S.-owned company, has been providing innovative transportation services for over 130 years. With operations spanning 36 nations and territories, the company is recognized as one of the leading employers of U.S. mariners. As Crowley Maritime embarks on this new chapter, stakeholders will be closely monitoring the impact of these changes on the company's growth trajectory and operational capabilities.

+ CROWLEY AND SEACOR JOIN FORCES IN NEW JONES ACT TANKER VENTURE

Crowley Maritime and SEACOR Holdings, through its subsidiary Seabulk Tankers, have formed a joint venture called Fairwater Holdings to integrate their liquid energy and chemical transportation assets. This collaboration aims to address the evolving demands of the U.S. domestic market. The agreement was finalized on 2nd January 2026, establishing Fairwater Holdings as an independent U.S. Jones Act service provider in the maritime sector.The new entity will amalgamate the strengths of both companies, providing a suite of transportation solutions across the petroleum and chemical trades, alongside third-party ship management services. Fairwater Holdings will manage a fleet comprising 20 ocean-going articulated tug-barges and 11 tankers, primarily under long-term charters. Furthermore, the joint venture will offer crewing and technical management for an additional 21 vessels owned by third parties, thereby enhancing its operational capacity and service offerings.Daniel Thorogood, currently the CEO of Seabulk, will take on the CEO role at Fairwater Holdings upon completion of the deal. With over a decade of experience leading Seabulk and managing SEACOR's short-sea shipping and logistics investments, Thorogood is expected to steer the new venture towards innovation and sustainable practices. In his statement, he emphasized that Fairwater marks a significant milestone in delivering safe and efficient maritime transportation solutions tailored to the needs of the U.S. energy sector.Tom Crowley, Chairman and CEO of Crowley Maritime, expressed confidence in the partnership, describing Seabulk as an ideal collaborator with a proven track record in the maritime industry. He noted that the joint venture aligns with Crowley's growth strategy, further solidifying its position as a leader in the U.S. maritime market, where it operates the largest American-flag petroleum and chemical tank vessel fleet, boasting a capacity exceeding 12 million barrels.As both companies work towards integrating their assets and services, the joint venture is expected to commence operations after meeting customary closing conditions and securing regulatory approvals. This integration effort is anticipated to create a unified and seamless transition for customers, ensuring uninterrupted support for their energy supply chains. Fairwater Holdings is set to begin operations in the first quarter of 2024, marking an exciting new chapter in U.S. maritime transportation.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ MAWANI STRENGTHENS TRADE TIES WITH CMA CGM’S NEW ROUTE

RIYADH: Maritime connectivity between Saudi Arabia and key Asian and European markets is set to expand after the Kingdom added CMA CGM’s 'Ocean Rise' shipping service to Jeddah Islamic Port. This new route, which commenced on the 3rd of May 2026, connects Jeddah Islamic Port with Kobe, Nagoya, and Yokohama in Japan; Xiamen, Yantian, and Nansha in China; Rotterdam in the Netherlands; Hamburg in Germany; and Southampton in the UK.According to Saudi Arabia’s Ports Authority, known as Mawani, the service has a handling capacity of up to 3,001 twenty-foot equivalent units (TEUs) and is aimed at supporting the movement of Saudi exports and imports in cooperation with international shipping lines. This addition is part of Mawani’s ongoing strategy to enhance the Kingdom’s global logistics capabilities.Mawani stated that this new service aligns with its efforts to enhance Saudi Arabia’s ranking in global performance indicators and support global trade flows. The move is in line with the objectives of the National Transport and Logistics Strategy, which aims to reinforce the Kingdom’s standing as a global logistics hub connecting three continents.During the current period, Mawani has launched 18 maritime shipping services, including the latest CMA CGM offering, with a combined estimated handling capacity of 123,552 TEUs. This expansion aims to ensure efficient delivery of goods to markets and enhance operational readiness across Saudi ports in cooperation with key global shipping companies, including MSC, Maersk, PIL, and Hapag-Lloyd.The Kingdom’s port activity has continued to expand, with Saudi ports recording a 6.66 percent annual increase in cargo throughput in March. Mawani-overseen ports handled 667,882 TEUs in February, marking a significant 20.89 percent increase from the same month a year earlier. Jeddah Islamic Port remains one of the Kingdom’s key maritime gateways, equipped with advanced facilities to bolster its capacity for international trade.

+ CMA CGM EXPANDS FLEET WITH TWO MORE VESSELS REGISTERED IN INDIA

CMACGM is transferring two more containerships into the Indian registry, according to officials in India. This development was reported by the Maritime Executive on April 29th, 2026, and marks an important step in the shipping company's strategy to enhance its presence in the Indian market.The shipping giant began moving vessels under the Indian flag last year to align with revised cabotage regulations. Earlier in April 2026, the CMA CGM Vila do Conde, a 33,434-dwt vessel built in 2009 with a capacity of 2,556 TEU, was officially transferred from the Maltese flag to India. This vessel has been part of CMACGM's fleet since 2023.Papers have also been filed for the transfer of the CMA CGM Semarang, a vessel constructed in 2007 with a deadweight tonnage of 38,000 and a capacity of 2,700 TEU. These two new transfers will bring the total number of vessels moved into the Indian registry by CMACGM to six, following the earlier transfers of the CMA CGM Victoria, CMA CGM Mendelssohn, CMA CGM Diamond, and CMA CGM Manaus.This initiative is largely a response to India's recent changes in cabotage regulations, which now restrict foreign-flagged vessels from carrying domestic cargo. Since January 2026, the Ministry of Ports, Shipping, and Waterways has rolled back a previous exemption that permitted foreign vessels to transship certain cargoes. CMA CGM's proactive approach to register vessels in India allows the company to navigate these new rules effectively while bolstering the domestic shipping industry.CMA CGM was the first major carrier to transition vessels into the Indian registry, with competitors like Maersk and MSC Mediterranean Shipping Company following suit. The new regulations also necessitate that registered vessels employ Indian seafarers, a move CMA CGM has already begun, employing 1,000 Indian seafarers with plans to increase this number to 1,500 by year-end. Furthermore, CMA CGM signed a contract with Cochin Shipyard in February 2026 for the construction of new vessels powered by LNG, reflecting its commitment to growth and sustainability in the region.

+ CMACGM'S AI-FIRST APPROACH TO REDEFINING INDIA'S LOGISTICS LANDSCAPE

As supply chains grow more volatile and customer expectations more exacting, logistics is undergoing a profound reinvention. CMA CGM Group outlines how India is emerging as both a strategic growth market and a global innovation hub, while AI, ecosystem partnerships, and digital platforms are redefining everything from port operations to customer experience. The result: a shift from reactive logistics to predictive, intelligent, and increasingly autonomous supply chains.On 28th April 2026, CMA CGM highlighted its commitment to harnessing artificial intelligence to enhance operational efficiencies within the logistics sector. The company is focusing on developing AI-driven solutions that optimize supply chain management, thereby minimizing disruptions caused by unpredictable market dynamics. By leveraging advanced analytics and machine learning, CMA CGM aims to create a more resilient logistics framework that meets the evolving demands of its customers.CMACGM's strategy involves forging partnerships with Indian tech companies to build a robust ecosystem that supports the integration of AI into logistics operations. This collaborative approach not only accelerates innovation but also positions India as a critical player in the global logistics landscape. The company's vision is to establish AI as a core component of its operational model, facilitating smarter decision-making and improved customer service.In addition to AI integration, CMA CGM is investing in digital platforms that enhance transparency and facilitate real-time tracking of shipments. This technological advancement empowers businesses to make informed decisions, thereby improving overall supply chain performance. As CMACGM continues to evolve its logistics capabilities, the emphasis is placed on creating a seamless experience for clients, ultimately driving growth in the e-commerce and manufacturing sectors.With CMA CGM at the forefront of this transformation, the logistics industry is poised for significant change. The company's focus on an AI-first strategy underscores the importance of innovation in maintaining competitive advantage. As global trade continues to evolve, CMACGM's initiatives in India may serve as a blueprint for other logistics providers aiming to navigate the complexities of modern supply chains.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

value.Title: Silicon Valley entrepreneur's latest company targets pet wellness market South Florida's tech & innovation news for the week of May 8
value.Overview: South Florida Business Journal (Fort Lauderdale/Miami, Florida), 895words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-6SB3-SB4K-014C-00000-00Silicon Valley entrepreneur's latest company targets pet wellness market2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:39:51ZLexisNexisSilicon Valley entrepreneur's latest company targets pet wellness marketSouth Florida's tech & innovation news for the week of May 8Ashley Portero

Dog owners can't stop spending money on their fluffy friends - and that's an opportunity for premium food brands.

Enter Golden Child. The Miami-based company officially launched last month after raising $37 million in seed funding from investors, including venture studio Atomic and venture capital firm Redpoint Ventures.

It's another business co-founded by serial entrepreneur Jack Abraham, managing partner and CEO of Atomic, and co-founder of publicly traded telehealth firm Hims & Hers. He teamed up with former Hims & Hers VP of Product Quentin Lacornerie to create Golden Child, which makes custom meals and "drizzle" kibble toppers for dogs.

"Our founding team, which includes veterinary nutrition and supply chain experts, has taken a thoughtful approach from the onset to making dog food that is of top quality, focused on digestibility, nutrition, and taste," Lacornerie said in a statement.

Abraham was among the California entrepreneurs who moved to Miami during the Covid-19 pandemic, a trend that brought national attention to SouthFlorida's tech economy. He is also the co-founder of Miami-based Exowatt, a renewable energy company developing modular solar energy storage units tailored for AI data centers.

Pet owners are spending increasing sums on their dogs and cats, especially on health and wellness aimed at boosting longevity. In the U.S. alone, pet owners spent about $158 billion in 2024, according to the American Pet Products Association. The fresh dog food segment, which stresses specialized, nutrient-dense meals, is contributing to that spending.

Golden Child is the latest pet industry company to call SouthFlorida home. Last July, The Farmer's Dog, a popular subscription dog food company with more than $1 billion in annual revenue, opened an office in Boca Raton. Chewy, the online pet supplies retailer and growing veterinary clinic operator, is headquartered in Plantation.

Fla. expands push to build competitive companies

The Florida Council of 100 wants to change the way the state competes for companies.

To do that, it is expanding its Ambition Accelerated initiative to attract and scale businesses in two key industries: healthcare, and climate resilience and infrastructure. It builds on the success of the Gold Coast Tech Accelerator, a program to build defense and financial technology companies in South Florida.

"Gold Coast Tech showed what's possible when you connect founders directly into Florida's economy," Florida Council of 100 President and CEO Mike Simas said. "This next phase is about scaling that model statewide."

While Florida is becoming a magnet for headquarters relocations, the goal of Ambition Accelerated is to position the state as a top destination for building businesses from the ground up. To do that, the state has to create a market that founders and operators want to be a part of.

The expanded model connects founders directly with customers, capital and industry leaders, guided by CEOs of major Florida companies.

Ambition Accelerated builds on the council's strategy outlined in its Beyond Sunshine report, which details a plan to leverage regional strengths to add 200,000 high-wage jobs in Florida by 2030.

SouthFlorida to be first link in quantum computing network

Florida LambdaRail is teaming up with Maryland-based IonQ to build a statewide communications network that can protect data from threats posed by quantum computing.

The project's first step will include a nearly 100-mile corridor that will link research and education institutions in the tri-county region.

Jason Bell, chair of the Florida LambdaRail board of directors, said the initiative will enable universities and researchers to move quantum computing from the lab to "real-world deployment."

"We're confident that this public and private project will be a catalyst to accelerate scalable quantum-secure connectivity across the state," he said.

Florida LambdaRail operates a 1,540-mile high-speed fiber-optic network owned by its 13 university equity partners to support research and data-intensive projects. Partners include the University of Miami, Florida International University, Florida Atlantic University and Nova Southeastern University.

The quantum network will build on Florida LambdaRail's existing fiber optic infrastructure to connect universities, government agencies and research institutions statewide. It will be designed to protect data against quantum computers - advanced machines that can solve problems that are too difficult for conventional computers - that can break current encryption methods.

Florida aims to establish itself as a leader in quantum computing and cybersecurity. Silicon Valley's D-Wave recently revealed it will move its headquarters to Boca Raton by the end of 2026. FAU also signed a $20 million deal to purchase a quantum computer from the company.

Big Number

$22M

What Fort Lauderdale-based Z Squared plans to pay in stock to acquire Skycore Digital LLC, which operates three active data center sites in North Carolina

value.Title: China’s $3 Billion US clean tech exit is an investment warning

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK2-XX43-S6MT-Y009-00000-00China’s $3 Billion US clean tech exit is an investment warning2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:54ZLexisNexisChina’s $3 Billion US clean tech exit is an investment warningNew Delhi


Renewable energy manufacturer Jinko Solar Co.’s recent decision to sell control of its Florida facility extends a multi-billion dollar retreat from the US by China’s clean technology firms, as they contend with an increasingly hostile policy environment and the potential loss of Biden-era incentives.



China-based companies in the sector scrapped about $2.8 billion in planned US manufacturing projects in 2025, according to research by Rhodium Group. As of the end of March, more than half of proposed Chinese clean-tech investments in the US announced since 2022 had been canceled, paused or delayed, according to the group's calculations.

That's part of a broader downturn that saw a 17% decline last year in all clean technology investment in the US, Rhodium said in a report published Wednesday.

Producers of solar equipment, batteries and electric vehicle technology have experienced a sharp reversal since Biden-era tax credits lured Chinese companies to announce $5.6 billion of investments in 2023 alone. Since then, President Donald Trump’s administration has rolled back incentives and, most crucially, last year’s tax bill introduced new hurdles for manufacturers with ties to so-called foreign entities of concern. 

“Jinko’s decision underscores the enormous challenges facing Chinese clean-tech firms operating in the US,” said Li Shuo, director of the China Climate Hub at the Asia Society Policy Institute. The company’s move should be seen as “a chilling message to anyone that wishes to come and build factories in the US,” he said.

Shanghai-based Jinko on Friday agreed to sell about a 75% stake in its solar panel facility in Florida to FH Capital, a private equity fund. The main purpose of selling the stake is “to optimize its overseas asset allocation, ensure its long-term strategic layout in the US, enhance flexibility and compliance, and facilitate its long-term development," a Jinko spokesperson said in a written response to Bloomberg questions.

The decision was prompted by a need to comply with “US domestic manufacturing regulations” and to “minimize operational risks,” Jinko said in a corporate filing, without citing any specific regulations. 

Jinko’s sell-down follows similar moves by China-based competitors to scale back exposure to the US or to exit entirely. Trina Solar Co. sold a majority stake in its Texas assembly facility in 2024, and last year Corning Inc. acquired a JA Solar Technology Co. plant in Arizona.

Shanghai-listed Ningbo Boway Alloy Material Co. said Wednesday it will sell solar manufacturing assets in the US to India’s INOXGFL Group due to the tightening of foreign entity requirements under Trump’s tax bill.

Policy changes under Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act mean it has become harder, if not completely impossible, for factories controlled by Chinese companies, or heavily reliant on China-dominated supply chains, to be eligible for lucrative manufacturing tax credits. 

Losing access to those credits puts Chinese-owned factories at a “huge disadvantage” compared to domestic rivals, said Rob Barnett, a senior analyst at Bloomberg Intelligence. For example, Arizona-based First Solar Inc., the largest US solar producer, told investors in February that it expects to receive more than $2 billion in credits this year.

While Treasury Department guidance on specific ownership thresholds for tax credit eligibility isn’t expected to be issued until later this year, analysts expect those conditions to be difficult for China-linked firms to meet.

“The policy environment is getting more restrictive,” said Margaret Jackson, a senior associate at the Center for Strategic and International Studies and previously a senior counselor for policy at US Department of Commerce during Biden’s tenure. 

Those tougher rules mean Trump’s meeting this week with President Xi Jinping in Beijing is unlikely to prompt new investments from China’s manufacturers in green technology industries. That’s even though Trump himself has at times expressed openness to the idea. 

“I’m not sure that below him there’s a lot of appetite to create space for more Chinese investment,” Jackson said.

--With assistance from Ocean Hou and Mark Chediak.

(Adds details on company selling US assets in 9th paragraph)
